docs.title: home
docs.text:
Topicalia is a small publishing experiment for language learning.

It produces short PDF courselets, built around topical lessons and recurring voices. A lesson begins with a word, a scene or a small cultural idea, which then starts to stir its classroom into creative trouble. Yes, its classroom, not your classroom - it already exists when the audience arrives.

A typical courselet comes as student and teacher editions - with a twist.

  • Student edition

    short lessons, voice reactions, class chronicles and appendices for student roster and patterns noticed along the way

  • Teacher edition

    answer keys, teaching notes and observations on class behaviour

a small editorial cast reacts to the lesson from different angles here is how the teacher gets involved

docs.title: first demo
docs.text:
Japan 1 courselet presents an English class on B2 level and features five lessons built around Japanese words: tsundoku, wabi-sabi, ikigai, omotenashi and kintsugi.

download Japan 1 student edition (PDF)

download Japan 1 teacher edition (PDF)

Both editions are intentionally short. The current showcase consists of five lessons. The point is not to cover English, but to demonstrate the Topicalia approach.

docs.title: lesson structure
docs.text:
A Topicalia lesson is brief by design but it gets new lives when the core part introduces the topic and leaves, passing the ball to Topicalia class.

lesson
  -> follow-up question
  -> persona reactions
  -> mood reactions
  -> class chronicle
  -> teacher notes on lesson
  -> teacher comments about selected class reactions

docs.title: the class
docs.text:
Personas are recurring language critics. Each one notices something different:

  • Ines collects living phrasework and distrusts polished sentences nobody would actually say.

  • Camila treats memory like an archive and watches how speakers reconstruct the past.

  • Piotr follows connectors, contrast and the small bridges that stop arguments collapsing.

  • Rafa pressure-tests language against ordinary life.

Other voices may join the board too: Martha qualifies certainty, Claire retells stories, Samir explores the paths opened by if and Ethan keeps rewriting until a sentence simply stays with you.

Moods are different. They are brief emotional readings of the same lesson: amused, analytical, bored, confused, impressed, irritated, sad and twisted. They arrive, leave a mark and disappear.

read more about what influenced this approach

Entire class roster is available as first appendix.

meet personas... ...and moods

docs.title: appendices
docs.text:
Topicalia marks selected language features inside the content. Those marks can later become appendices: idioms, connectors, grammar areas, lesson types and other patterns that would otherwise remain scattered.

The rule is deliberately freeform. An appendix should collect useful language, not every shiny object that impresses on the language journey.

highlights become indexes when the dust settles

docs.title: what comes next
docs.text:
The next planned courselet is Brasil 1, aimed to bring Brazilian words and the peculiar beauty of Portuguese. It will also awaken Martha character, who has been waiting politely near the subjunctive zone and may finally get busy with her beloved grammar structure.

Also planned is a follow-up to Japan 1, which will zoom in rather than move on, with a whole new unit around a single word, gaman, exploring patience, endurance, pressure, silence and self-control.

docs.title: behind the scenes
docs.text:
Topicalia data is stored as HTML fragments, organized with JSON structures and transformed into PDF files by a small Python/Flask toolchain, using WeasyPrint as final renderer.

While content development uses AI assistance heavily, the final material is edited and curated by hand.

docs.title: why gonzo
docs.text:
The term inevitably points back to Hunter S. Thompson, whose name is inseparable from gonzo journalism - a form of reporting that places the reporter inside the story and, rather than pretending to stand at a neutral distance, moves through it with a mix of subjectivity, participation, satire and exaggeration that makes the act of observing indistinguishable from the act of altering.

That lineage matters, though only up to a point. Topicalia is not interested in imitating Thompson's prose, his politics or his persona so much as in borrowing something quieter and more structural - the sense that the observer alters what is being observed simply by being there and that voice is not an accessory to information but part of it.

Commentary is part of the event.

There is also a second, less deliberate lineage behind the name: Gonzo from The Muppet Show, a character who never quite settles into the category assigned to him and who performs acts that are unnecessary, excessive, occasionally absurd and carried out with little concern for whether they will be understood at all. He does not clarify situations so much as intensifies them, pushing things slightly off balance, making them stranger and therefore easier to notice. That impulse - toward the unnecessary but revealing gesture - has its place here as well, where not every voice is required to explain and some are allowed to distort, exaggerate or simply remain at an angle to the lesson, which is often where meaning begins to show itself.

docs.title: the second inheritance
docs.text:
Anthony Bourdain belongs here for a different reason. He was not a gonzo journalist in any strict sense but he practiced a related kind of presence, moving through food, cities, eateries and local customs without much interest in appearing objective.

What matters here is not cuisine or celebrity but method: the way he entered spaces, listened, reacted, digressed, judged, admired and occasionally recoiled, without ever suggesting that the world was waiting to be arranged into neat pedagogical categories. That rhythm - not polished explanation but situated presence - is close to what Topicalia draws from its auxiliary voices.

docs.title: what gonzo means inside Topicalia
docs.text:
A lesson may say one thing plainly, a persona may restate it more diplomatically and a mood may push it toward something harsher, stranger, or unintentionally revealing; the core content remains stable but the field around it shifts just enough to make the same material behave differently under pressure.

This is why the word "gonzo" still earns its place, suggesting that the lesson is never quite a sealed object but something unfolding in the presence of voices that observe, interfere and occasionally distort it as it takes shape.

docs.title: further reading
docs.text:
If you want to follow the thread a bit further:

  • Uncovering the Origins of "Gonzo" on the 20th Anniversary of Hunter S. Thompson’s Death
  • Anthony Bourdain - Parts Unknown
  • Anthony Bourdain - selected interviews
  • Gonzo - The Muppet Show clips
